chunk_summary: Vitamin A prophylaxis and treatment are critical for stunted children to prevent deficiency-related complications and support linear growth, with specific dosing schedules and therapeutic regimens outlined for different age groups.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vitamin A in preventing stunting and infection risk.
Learn the prophylactic dosing schedule for vitamin A in children aged 9 months to 5 years.
Recognize clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency and appropriate therapeutic regimens.
Identify precautions and counseling points for caregivers regarding vitamin A supplementation.
key_facts: Prophylactic vitamin A dosing includes 100,000 IU at 9 months and 200,000 IU every 6 months from 16–18 months to 5 years.
Therapeutic regimen for deficiency: 50,000 IU for <6 months, 100,000 IU for 6–11 months, and 200,000 IU for ≥12 months, repeated on day 1, day 2, and at 2 weeks.
Clinical signs of deficiency include night blindness, conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, and corneal changes.
Vitamin A supplementation is often given with measles-containing vaccines at 9 months.
Transient toxicity symptoms include vomiting and irritability; caregivers should be counseled on when to return for follow-up.
reference_quote: Program schedules describe nine prophylactic doses from 9 months to 5 years — National vitamin A supplementation program.
document_key_topics: Vitamin A prophylaxis schedule for children
Therapeutic management of vitamin A deficiency
Clinical signs and treatment of deficiency-related complications
Counseling caregivers on vitamin A supplementation
